title: Hormuz Shut Again as Iran-US Talks Head to Switzerland, Fed Hawks Circle
subhead: Iran re-closed the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, citing Israeli attacks in Lebanon and US bad faith, even as negotiators from both sides converged on Switzerland. The Fed's new chair signaled a rate hike by year-end, and the G7 took aim at China's mineral grip while Russia's fuel crisis deepened.

sections.heading: Hormuz Closed Again as Peace Deal Frays
sections.body:
Iran's joint military command announced Saturday that the Strait of Hormuz has been closed again, citing continued Israeli attacks in Lebanon and what it called US bad faith in implementing the 14-point memorandum of understanding. The move came just days after ships had begun transiting the strait following the interim US-Iran deal signed earlier in the week.

Iran's IRGC-linked Tasnim News Agency reported that the strait should remain shut until all MoU conditions are met — including the release of at least $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets, implementation of oil sanctions waivers, and Israel's withdrawal from Lebanon. The statement called reopening Hormuz solely in exchange for lifting the US naval blockade a strategic mistake.

US Central Command pushed back, stating that 55 merchant ships transited the strait on Saturday, moving over 17 million barrels of oil, and that safe passage remained intact. President Trump posted on Truth Social that there will be NO TOLLS in the Strait of Hormuz during or after the 60-day ceasefire — except tolls imposed by the United States 'for services rendered as the Guardian Angel to the countries of the Middle East.'

Former Pentagon official Mick Mulroy told ABC News that Iran believes it holds the upper hand, with the power to choke off a fifth of global energy supply — a lever it did not exercise before the war. He noted Iran is already collecting sanctions relief before substantive nuclear talks have even begun.
sections.whyItMatters: The deal that was supposed to stabilize energy markets is unraveling in real time. Iran is using Hormuz as leverage to extract more concessions, while the US insists shipping continues — creating a dangerous gap between rhetoric and reality on the water.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks Restart as Deal Shows Strain
sections.body:
Despite the Hormuz closure, diplomatic channels remain open. Vice President JD Vance left for Switzerland on Saturday for talks with Iranian representatives, telling reporters he hoped to make progress on nuclear issues and the Lebanon ceasefire. Negotiators Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner are already on the ground in Switzerland, according to Vance, handling technical elements of the negotiation.

Iran's negotiating team also departed for Switzerland on Saturday, Iranian state television reported. The talks were originally scheduled for Friday but were delayed — likely due to the Hormuz closure announcement and the Lebanon escalation.

The deal is under pressure from multiple sides. Iran's Tasnim outlet says reopening Hormuz requires fulfilling all MoU conditions, not just one. Senate Armed Services Chairman Roger Wicker called the proposed $300 billion economic development fund for Iran a payoff that makes Obama's 2015 deal look like a pittance. Vance disagreed, saying critics assume Iran gets benefits without changing behavior — something he said is not in the agreement's text.

Meanwhile, Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed at least 16 people on Saturday, including two children, according to Lebanon's National News Agency. Hezbollah said it would abide by the ceasefire but repel Israeli attacks. Neither Israel nor Hezbollah are signatories to the US-Iran deal.
sections.whyItMatters: The diplomacy is still alive but under simultaneous pressure from Iran's maximalist demands, Israel's military campaign in Lebanon, and domestic US political criticism. The coming 48 hours in Switzerland will determine whether this deal holds or collapses.

sections.heading: Fed's Warsh Debut Signals Hike Path as Inflation Stays Hot
sections.body:
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh presided over his first policy meeting on Wednesday, keeping rates steady but sending a distinctly hawkish signal. The updated dot plot showed half the committee leaning toward a rate hike by year-end. Warsh declined to share his own rate outlook at the press conference, but two-year Treasury yields surged 13 basis points in post-press-conference trading — the largest jump since April 2025.

US inflation stands at a three-year high, with PCE at 3.8% year-on-year in April, and forecasters expect May's PCE data, due Thursday, to show further acceleration on both monthly and annual bases. Bloomberg Economics noted that even though Warsh didn't submit his own dot, his tone at the news conference seemed notably hawkish, and a hot PCE reading would reinforce that message.

Markets have repriced aggressively. UBS pushed its expected easing timeline to March 2027, meaning no cuts this year. Money markets now fully price a 25bp hike by October, a dramatic shift from where expectations stood weeks ago.

The coming week features keynote remarks from New York Fed President John Williams on June 25 and Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee the same day. Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari appears on a panel June 26 — all watched for signals on the rate path.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh's debut made clear the Fed is not waiting for the Iran deal to cool inflation. If PCE confirms the upward trend on Thursday, a rate hike by year-end goes from likely to near-certain — tightening financial conditions precisely when the global economy is absorbing the Iran shock.

sections.heading: Gold Under Pressure as Dollar and Yields Rise
sections.body:
Spot gold is in a downtrend, caught between the Fed's hawkish pivot and the Iran deal's de-escalation signal. Gold dropped 2.87% for the week, contributing to India's $10 billion reserve decline, and is now testing a key retracement zone at $4,203 to $4,161 per ounce. The June 11 main bottom sits at $4,024.

According to FXEmpire analysis, gold's 200-day moving average sits at $4,463 and the 50-day at $4,541 — both well above current prices, confirming the bearish structure. The next inflation data release will determine whether buyers defend the retracement zone or the selloff accelerates toward the June low.

The dollar index hit a one-year high on the combination of rate-hike expectations and structural demand from the Iran deal's dollar-denominated financial architecture. The inverse correlation between gold and real yields has reasserted itself after months of war-driven divergence.
sections.whyItMatters: Gold's pullback reflects a market recalibrating from crisis pricing to a hawkish Fed reality. If inflation data comes in hot this week, the gold floor will be tested — and the dollar's strength compounds pressure on emerging-market currencies already burning reserves.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance Takes On China's 70% Refining Dominance
sections.body:
The G7 summit in Evian produced a significant supply-chain initiative: the G7 Critical Minerals Resilience and Production Alliance. The declaration committed members to coordinating efforts to diversify critical minerals supply chains, build alternative mining, processing, and refining capacity, and reduce dependence on any single supplier. It expressed concern about non-market policies, arbitrary export restrictions, and economic coercion — language clearly aimed at Beijing without naming China.

According to OilPrice, citing IEA data, China controls roughly 70% of global refining capacity for critical minerals and dominates 19 of 20 minerals the IEA analyzes. In rare earths specifically, China holds a 94% share of permanent magnet manufacturing, up from 50% two decades ago. The US is developing a rare-earth price floor mechanism to protect markets from Chinese manipulation, though the G7 declaration lists it as under consideration rather than a commitment.

A US congressional investigation found that China's legal framework for mineral price reporting effectively makes it illegal to publish prices that deviate from government wishes — giving Beijing the ability to raise and lower prices to serve its strategic interests.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is putting real diplomatic weight behind supply-chain diversification, but China has grown its market share in recent years despite Western efforts. The gap between declarations and physical alternative capacity remains enormous — 94% magnet manufacturing share cannot be displaced by policy statements alone.

sections.heading: Russia's Fuel Crisis Deepens as Ukrainian Drones Hit Refineries
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone strikes are inflicting measurable damage on Russia's domestic fuel supply. A Ukrainian drone attack on Tuesday started a fire at the largest oil refinery supplying the Moscow region, halting operations, according to Reuters. On Saturday, President Zelenskiy confirmed Ukrainian drones struck a refinery in western Siberia's Tyumen region — extending the reach of Ukraine's energy campaign deep into Russian territory.

Russia has responded by slashing fuel quality standards for the domestic market, allowing refineries to produce fuel with lower environmental specifications, Kommersant reported on June 15. The move is a direct response to Ukrainian attacks on energy infrastructure squeezing domestic supply.

The IEA confirmed that Russian crude production fell roughly 10% below May targets to 8.7 million barrels per day, and approximately 5% year-on-year — the first such decline attributed directly to Ukrainian attacks. Trump signaled a swift return of sanctions on Russian oil at the G7, and the UK sanctioned the banking arm of Yandex, two other lenders, and dozens of vessels in Russia's shadow fleet. Canada announced fresh sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet and energy revenues.
sections.whyItMatters: The combination of Ukrainian drone strikes and tightening sanctions is squeezing Russia's fuel production and export capacity. The fuel quality downgrade is a sign of real domestic pressure — and a reminder that financial warfare and kinetic warfare are now operating on the same target.

sections.heading: UK Eases Lukoil Restrictions as Global Rate-Hike Wave Persists
sections.body:
In a quieter but notable development, the UK sanctions authority relaxed financial restrictions on Lukoil International and its subsidiaries, allowing payments the temporary licence had previously blocked. The move is limited in scope but significant — it signals that even as the G7 tightens sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet, some Western jurisdictions are easing pressure on specific Russian energy entities, likely to avoid collateral damage to European energy markets.

Meanwhile, the global tightening wave continues. The Bank of Japan raised to 1% — the highest since 1995 — with Deputy Governor Himino speaking Wednesday and board member Tamura on Thursday. The Bank of Canada held rates steady but faces accelerating inflation at 3% in May. Australia's trimmed mean CPI quickened to 3.4% in May, with RBA official Andrew Hauser speaking later this week. The ECB hiked last week and keeps a July move on the table, with Lagarde testifying to the European Parliament on Monday. Brazil cut its rate to 14.25%, though the post-decision statement confused analysts and raised credibility questions.

Across the board, central banks are treating the Iran-war inflation shock as structural rather than transient — even as the deal itself begins to ease energy prices in futures markets.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lukoil easing shows the limits of sanctions cohesion — even allies carving out exceptions when domestic energy costs bite. And the rate-hike cascade across every major bloc means the financial impact of the Iran war will persist well beyond the ceasefire, tightening global financial conditions into a slowdown.

bottomLine: Saturday's Hormuz re-closure turned a week of cautious optimism into a reminder that the Iran deal is fragile and reversible. Iran is demanding $12 billion in asset releases and Israeli withdrawal before reopening the strait — conditions far beyond what the MoU promised. Negotiators head to Switzerland anyway, but the gap between the deal's text and the reality on the water is widening. Meanwhile, the Fed's Warsh made clear that rate hikes are back on the table, PCE data due Thursday could confirm the hawkish case, and central banks worldwide are tightening into a slowdown that the Iran shock created but the Iran deal may not fix. Gold is testing key support, the dollar is at a one-year high, and the G7 is trying to build alternative mineral supply chains that will take years to materialize. The war's financial consequences haven't ended — they've just shifted from crisis mode to slow-burn mode.
sources: USA Today, Arab Times Online, Yahoo News/ABC News, Shillong Times, OilPrice, FXEmpire, Business Standard, Reuters, Swiss Info, Kommersant, IEA, Bloomberg, RTE, Seeking Alpha
